title: Sleep Gate
author: Mrs. Reporter
excerpt: Team Ragebait is facing fresh controversy after a leaked voice memo appeared to capture coach Sparklemouse sleeping during a crucial Game 3 against Keyboard Warriors.
pullQuote: "Did you win?". A dismaying statement from one of the highest paid coaches of the VCC.
officialStatement: I am not currently able to comment on the coaching I provided to Team Ragebait during game 3 as the strategies and rehearsed plays are protected under Team Ragebait Trademark and I have signed an NDA. However, I can report that sometimes as a coach, it is appropriate to take a more hands-off approach and to allow your players to showcase their skills in strategy and execution. I trusted Team Ragebait to clutch the win and they followed through. I can also comment that getting a good sleep is essential in e-sports for all players and coaches in order to thrive at peak performance during games.
content:
Mired in controversy once again, Team Ragebait has a new scandal on the horizon: Sleep Gate.

In a nail biter of a match against Keyboard Warriors, Team Ragebait hoped to rely on their trusty coach, Sparklemouse to unleash a wicked gameplan in Game 3. Unfortunately, it has been discovered through a leaked voice memo that Sparklemouse was quite literally, sleeping on the job. In the memo, clear audio can be heard of a conversation between Ellie and Strump, lead forwards for Team Ragebait. Below is a transcript from the memo:

Ellie: "Alright Sparklemouse, what do you think of Strump killing Scadutree Avatar to close out our game?"

Sparklemouse: *Silence*

Strump: "Oh she must be locked in, really brainstorming on what Keyboard Warriors could do to close the game."

Ellie: "Naw, she's probably asleep. It was an early morning after all preparing for this game."

As the game came to a close, it can be heard on Ellie's stream that her mic was cut (turned off). Ellie, in a closed interview acknowledge to her sources that she called out to Sparklemouse. What she received would shock VCC fans and loyalists, but it must come out.

Sparklemouse: "What?!".

Now, this "What?!", may seem harmless, unremarkable, but looks do deceive. Ellie confirmed with us that she has heard this before. This "What?!" came from the voice of a coach that fell asleep and was startled as she woke up.

Need proof? We have more coms between the players to share:

Ellie: "Hey Steph we're going to do post game interviews now."

Sparklemouse: "But I don't even know what happened for half of the game...did you win?"

Sparklemouse was asked to comment. Despite her hesitancy, this is what she provided:

"I am not currently able to comment on the coaching I provided to Team Ragebait during game 3 as the strategies and rehearsed plays are protected under Team Ragebait Trademark and I have signed an NDA. However, I can report that sometimes as a coach, it is appropriate to take a more hands-off approach and to allow your players to showcase their skills in strategy and execution. I trusted Team Ragebait to clutch the win and they followed through. I can also comment that getting a good sleep is essential in e-sports for all players and coaches in order to thrive at peak performance during games."

"Did you win?". A dismaying statement from one of the highest paid coaches of the VCC. Behaviour that is unquestionably immature and irresponsible. It is clear to anyone who values the INTRGERITY of Elden Ring E-Sports that she should be reprimanded and fined for her incredulity.

title: Interview: Coach Rambler of Dazed & Lazy
author: Mrs. Reporter
excerpt: Coach Rambler discusses contract rumors, Dazed & Lazy, Coach Lemon, The Book of War, and the speculation around Helios and Shaker.
content:
Q: You are the highest-paid coach in the VCC right now according to your team, Dazed & Lazy. How does it feel being so sought after?

A: My agent deals with details like that, on to Cincinatti.

Q: Why did you go with Dazed & Lazy after all the bidding wars between the teams were done?

A: The dream of any coach is to coach winners and they will be tired of winning before the end of my contract.

Q: The Book of War has been teased to the public for a while now. Will it ever reach the public's eyes or is it meant to be kept a secret for your students?

A: I spoke to my friend George RR Martin about it and we decided to switch responsibilities. He will answer about the Book of War, I will answer about Winds of Winter. Arya's chapters are almost done.

Q: Team Brand Risk is your next matchup. Is there any preparation to go up against the current VCC champion Coach Lemon?

A: I have enormous respect for Coach Lemon and his schemes, but there is a reason why the organizers had to change the rules after that tournament, right? Just saying, we are prepared for everything.

Q: Last but not least, there were rumors that Shaker was to be on the team instead of Helios. We actually don't know much about Helios and have never seen him and Shaker in the same room. Can you enlighten us on what went down last minute when the roster was finalized, because as we know the VCC coach has to sign off on the team?

A: I can neither confirm nor deny anything about this story except one thing: Shaker will be proud of Helios like I am every day in practice.

title: Vex Leaves Globo Purple, Eliminating Them from the VCC
author: Dr. Lau
excerpt: Vex’s exit from Globo Purple has shaken up the VCC playoff picture, with contract tensions, Shaker’s influence, and new superteam rumors now dominating the conversation.
pullQuote: With Globo Purple unable to meet Vex’s demands, the story has quickly shifted from one player’s rise to the possibility of Shaker building an entirely new power in the VCC.
content:
Vex’s breakout performance has now led to one of the biggest roster shocks of the playoffs, as his departure from Globo Purple has effectively eliminated the organization from VCC contention.

According to league rumors, Vex began looking for personal management after outperforming expectations, and after consulting with Shaker, pushed for a raise from Purple Cum Bugs that the organization could not provide. The development adds another chapter to a familiar story for Shaker, whose own first season ended with him being removed from a team that could no longer afford his salary.

What followed turned Shaker into one of the most feared names in the VCC, as he went on to win the championship three seasons in a row. Now, with speculation building around Vex’s future, rumors are growing that Shaker may be interested in teaming up with him. And with Shaker widely believed to have the resources to build an organization of his own, the possibility of a new power project entering the next VCC season is starting to feel very real.

title: Brand Risk vs Rick Fisters Interview
author: Mrs. Reporter
excerpt: A post-match interview with Brand Risk after one of the most decisive victories in VCC history.
content:
Reporter: I was given a few minutes to interview the players and coach of team Brand Risk after the most decisive victory the VCC has ever and possibly will ever see. These are their answers.

So coach Lemon, what was the key to winning these games?

Lemon: Well Ms. Reporter lady I strive for very tight and very clean execution from my players, and after game 1 at least one of my players needed to be executed. But we cleaned it up and had a super clean game 2 and it went as expected.

Reporter: Really? How did you expect the second game to be so one sided?

Lemon: That I cannot answer fully as per my NDA, but one of the players on team Rick Fisters enjoys nice steak dinners a bit too much.

Reporter: What…

Reporter: Alright, I’m here with the player Lemon said was the second most important part of the team, Thomas how are you feeling after today's games?

Thomas: My back hurts a lil but I gotta say having such a long break in the middle of game 1 was super nice and really well done by our coach.

Reporter: Um ok quick follow up what did Lemon have to do with Josh’s power outage?

Thomas: It was really nice to have such a long break and I can’t thank Lemon enough. My back really needed the rest after that opener.

Reporter: Ok and a closing statement from the last member of this team, Nartock are you exited to be moving on to the next round of the VCC Playoffs?

Nartock: Yes and no, I really love playing but my coach is doing a bit more than just being a DJ during our games. Vibes are still high but we are running out of songs from the 2010s.

Reporter: Alright well you heard it from Brand Risk, Lemon appears to be the reason not for the win but more the loss of team Rick Fisters.

title: The Bofa Dynasty Prepare's for Playoffs
author: Johnathan
excerpt: The tournament favorite has looked dominant so far, but can anyone stop Bofa Dynasty before they claim the VCC trophy?
pullQuote: It feels like my birth right to get the W. Nothing can stand in our way.
content:
It is no secret that coming into this tournament, The Bofa Dynasty was going to be the favorite.

Before a single board was drafted, before a single square was claimed, and before the rest of the league had even settled on whether they were contenders or just background noise, Bofa Dynasty had already made their intentions clear. They were not here to "make a run." They were not here to "see what happens." They were here to win the whole thing, and more importantly, to make sure everyone knew it.

The rumors started early. First came word that they had bought out a franchised spot for a number that several VCC insiders described as "immature". Then came the roster moves.

Securing Dino alone was enough to make the rest of the league nervous. A player who has built a reputation for turning even the messiest game states into wins, Dino has become one of those names that changes the tone of a room the second it is mentioned. Add in The Crimson Chin Doitz, a player whose confidence somehow exceeds even his own results page, and suddenly Bofa Dynasty stopped looking like a team and started looking like a warning.

And that was before the coaching move.

Because while most organizations look for stability, value, or perhaps a coach who can work within reasonable financial constraints, Bofa Dynasty decided to go in a different direction and bring in Doitz_Alt. Since then, league speculation has only intensified. Some believe this was the move that pushed the organization beyond its financial limits. Others believe the loan rumors are exaggerated. Still others believe there is no loan at all, and that the Bofa front office simply discovered a vault somewhere beneath the venue labeled "for emergencies only."

Whatever the truth may be, one thing is certain: this roster was not assembled cheaply, and it was not assembled to lose in the VCC.

So far, the investment has looked worth every questionable decision attached to it.

Bofa Dynasty tore through The Grand Line without dropping a match, and they did not do it in the shaky, lucky, barely-survived kind of way that weaker teams love to call "grit." They did it with control. They did it with pace. They did it with the kind of confidence that makes even close games feel inevitable. Every series seemed to follow the same script: opponents entered hopeful, stayed competitive just long enough to build tension, and then watched Bofa close the door without much ceremony.

Their drafts have looked polished. Their comms, by all available reports, have looked calm. Their ability to recover from bad openings has made them even more dangerous than teams that simply dominate from the start. And perhaps most importantly, they carry themselves like a group that fully expects the bracket to bend around them.

That mentality starts, as it so often does, with Dino.

Fresh off another tournament win, Dino enters the playoffs with the kind of form that turns confidence into inevitability. Every event seems to add to the legend, and every legend makes the next opponent just a little more nervous. It is becoming increasingly difficult to tell where the actual player ends and where the mythology begins.

We also got a quick word with Dino after yet another win and asked the question the rest of the bracket is getting tired of hearing.

“How does it feel to keep on winning?”

Dino did not hesitate.

“It feels like my birth right to get the W. Nothing can stand in our way.”

It was the kind of answer that will either age like prophecy or become bulletin-board material for every team left in the field. But for now, with Bofa Dynasty continuing to tear through the tournament, it is hard to argue with the confidence.

At this point, Dino is not just winning matches. Dino is becoming the answer people give when asked why their own team could not quite get it done.

But Bofa Dynasty is not just one player.

That is what makes them terrifying.

Doitz brings the kind of pressure that forces mistakes before the game even stabilizes. Doitz_Alt, whether through preparation, mind games, or whatever classified methods the organization is allegedly funding, has managed to turn this team into something more disciplined than its collection of personalities should reasonably allow. On paper, this roster should be loud, chaotic, and occasionally self-destructive. In practice, they have looked coordinated, direct, and brutally efficient.

That is not to say there are no questions.

There always are in playoffs.

Can they handle a true momentum swing if a series stops going their way? Can they recover if Dino finally has an off day? Can they survive a matchup where the other side is willing to drag them into uncomfortable drafts, ugly boards, and long, grinding wins instead of the clean showcases they seem to prefer? Dominance in groups is one thing. Playoff pressure is another. The deeper a tournament goes, the more every favorite discovers that being feared is not the same thing as being untouchable.

And yet, every time those questions come up, Bofa Dynasty answers them the same way: by winning again.

So now the rest of the VCC is left staring at the same question.

Is there anyone in this tournament that can actually stop them?

Can some underdog catch fire at the right moment? Can a disciplined opponent expose a weakness no one else has found yet? Can someone force Bofa Dynasty into a real crisis and prove that all the money, all the hype, and all the dynasty talk was just that?

Or are we watching the beginning of something much worse for the rest of the field?

Because if Bofa Dynasty lifts the VCC Trophy, this will not be remembered as a strong season or even a dominant run. It will be remembered as the moment the warnings became reality. The moment the spending, the rumors, the star power, and the results all aligned. The moment a team built to look inevitable finally became exactly that.

And if that happens, the rest of the league will have no choice but to accept what Bofa Dynasty has been trying to tell them from the start:

This was never a playoff push.

This was a takeover.

title: Is Ragebait secretly rich?
author: Mrs. Reporter
excerpt: What first looked like suspicious spending by Team Ragebait may actually be something far more embarrassing for the rest of the league: undisclosed coaching payments from rival teams trying to improve their chances in the VCC.
pullQuote: Sparklemouse’s response has turned Ragebait from primary suspect into something far more dangerous in the court of public opinion: the team that might walk out of a legal review looking not guilty, profitable, and smug.
officialStatement:
We understand that there may be some confusion around Team Ragebait's spending and budget. While it is not ideal to have to make a statement on this matter, transparency is necessary to resolve things with VCC's Legal Team.

I will confirm that there are undisclosed statements but they are in fact, statements of income rather than expenditure. Several VCC teams reached out to me for private coaching services to improve their chances in the VCC. The lack of transparency was a result of an attempt to shield these teams from the embarrassment of requesting coaching specifically for Erdtree Avatar fights.

Since I am a teacher, this was considered to be personal income. I am always very happy to support other players for the love of the game and in the spirit of good sportsmanship. And of course, the extra income was welcome in light of economic inflation and Ellie's gameplay injury against Renalla, who out-ragebaited her in a moment of rare weakness.

However, the teams I supported absolutely should have included these coaching sessions in their team budgets as this would align with VCC contract policy and would have prevented any confusion on this matter. We are inclined to believe these accusations may be an attempt from other teams in the VCC to disqualify Team Ragebait on false grounds. Team Ragebait has submitted a request with VCC's Legal team to investigate this further.
content:
Legal has officially gotten in touch with Team Ragebait after a series of suspicious transactions were reportedly seen drifting through VCC channels, private whispers, and whatever dark financial alley exists between “normal team operations” and “this absolutely needs to be reviewed.”

At first glance, nothing about Ragebait’s season was supposed to stand out financially.

Their budget submission came in looking modest. Respectable. Manageable, even. The kind of number that tells the league, “We are here to compete, but we are also grounded, reasonable people who understand fiscal responsibility.” It was not the kind of number that should have triggered scrutiny. It was not the kind of number that should have raised eyebrows. And yet, somehow, eyebrows are now fully raised.

Per VCC contracts, the owners of each spot are required to provide full transparency regarding the amount of money they intend to spend during the season. The rule is simple in theory and apparently much more complicated in practice: teams must declare their operating amount at the beginning of the season and cannot go over budget unless they receive explicit approval under the patches-clause.

The patches-clause, for those unfamiliar, exists to cover exceptional circumstances. A roster emergency. A sudden replacement. A legitimate competitive adjustment. It does not, according to multiple people who claim to have read the handbook, exist for “mysterious vibes-based expenditures” or “whatever Team Ragebait appears to be doing.”

And that is where things begin to get interesting.

Despite presenting themselves as a fairly humble operation, Team Ragebait has recently found itself at the center of speculation after several undisclosed financial statements allegedly connected to Coach Sparklemouse began circulating around the VCC. No one seems willing to say exactly where those statements came from. No one seems eager to explain why they were noticed in the first place. But once they appeared, the conversation shifted quickly from casual curiosity to full-blown suspicion.

Because here is the issue: the numbers do not seem to match the image.

Ragebait has not carried itself like a team drowning in luxury. There have been no wildly theatrical press reveals, no velvet-curtain roster unveilings, no open declarations that they are building a superteam in plain sight. If anything, they have leaned into the exact opposite. Quiet budget. Quiet expectations. Quiet presentation.

And that would all be very convincing if the paperwork had stayed quiet too.

Instead, what the league appears to have found are hints — perhaps small on their own, but much more interesting when stacked together. Enough to make people ask whether Team Ragebait’s declared budget was truly their budget, or simply the number they felt like writing down at the time. Enough to raise a much more dangerous possibility: that Ragebait may have been operating with resources that the rest of the league was never told about.

Now, to be perfectly clear, speculation is not proof. Suspicion is not a verdict. And unusual financial movement can sometimes be explained by something boring and administrative, which would be deeply unfortunate for everyone hoping for scandal.

However, this is the VCC, and boring explanations tend to lose ground quickly when a more entertaining theory is available.

And for a time, the theory making the rounds was simple: Team Ragebait may be much wealthier than they had let on.

Naturally, much of that speculation centered on Sparklemouse.

Coach Sparklemouse, to public knowledge, is a teacher, which is important for two reasons. First, because it makes them sound responsible. Second, because it immediately caused half the league to become amateur accountants. More than one observer pointed out that teachers are not generally known for casually funding unexplained competitive expenditures on the side. That does not mean it is impossible, of course. It simply means the numbers inspired questions, and those questions got louder.

So where, exactly, was the money coming from?

Was there outside backing that had never been properly disclosed? Was there an investor quietly floating behind the scenes? Did Ragebait discover some obscure interpretation of league finance rules that allowed them to move resources around in ways no one else thought to try? Or, perhaps most concerning of all, had the league simply missed it while Ragebait kept spending?

And if they were spending, what were they spending on?

That question, it turns out, may have had the wrong verb.

Because after growing speculation, we were able to get Coach Sparklemouse for comment on the situation. And according to Sparklemouse, the controversial statements were not records of spending at all. They were records of income.

And just like that, the story changed.

Sparklemouse’s response has turned Ragebait from primary suspect into something far more dangerous in the court of public opinion: the team that might walk out of a legal review looking not guilty, profitable, and smug.

For now, Team Ragebait remains under scrutiny, but the suspicion is no longer pointing in only one direction.

Because once legal starts pulling on a thread like this, there is no telling which teams, budgets, or Erdtree Avatar-related insecurities may come loose with it.

And if Sparklemouse is telling the truth, the question is no longer whether Ragebait was secretly rich.

It is how many other teams were secretly paying them.
